1.label: Question 1 (PMI Experience)
1.scenario: A health Insurance Company that is in its 4th year of operation has largely been selling Short term individual Private Medical Insurance (PMI) policies till date.

i.question: The company wants to perform an experience analysis on its portfolio. List down the possible areas of experience analysis and explain the method it should use to analyse each of these areas for PMI.

Morbidity Experience: Investigate by incidence and claim cost. Categories: product type, sales channel, hospital, procedure, age, gender, occupation, etc. [1]
PMI is indemnity-based; investigate large limits vs average claim amounts. [½]
Break down into: inpatient/day case/outpatient, hospital band, distribution source, etc. [½]
Focus on new procedures, drugs, or equipment impacts. [½]
Allow for delays in reporting (IBNR) and settlement. [½]
Expenses: Subdivide non-commission costs into initial, renewal, claim, and investment cells. [½]
Use timesheets for staff and pragmatic allocation for overheads (e.g., floor space). [½]
Investment Returns: Calculate rate of return using standard formula: I = A_t+1 - (A_t + CF_t) / (A_t + ½CF_t). [1]
PMI investment pool is typically small, so return significance is low. [½]
Lapse / Persistency: Lapse rates = number of lapses / exposed to risk. [½]
Subdivide by region, policy type, and channel; allow for grace periods. [½]
Analyze monthly by policy count or premium. [½]
New Business Mix: Compare actual vs expected volume and mix; deviation impacts profitability and capital. [1]

ii.question: What are practical challenges for an insurer in offering portability? Also, discuss how this could have impacted the experience of the Insurer?

Practical Challenges: Delay in getting response/history from previous insurer. [1]
System complications for partial SI portability and waiting period waivers. [1]
Underwriting complications: unclear SI for porting life. [½]
Cannot charge porting costs to policyholder. [½]
Customer dissatisfaction due to misunderstanding complex regulations. [½]
Impact on Experience: Selective lapsing: healthy lives porting out. [1]
Pricing challenges: cannot price porting vs new policies differently. [½]
Difficulty in projecting renewal assumptions and initial expense recovery. [½]

iii.question: List down the possible reasons for morbidity profits and expense losses.

Morbidity Profits: Sophisticated/health-conscious market mix compared to pricing. [1]
Selection effect: waiting periods for major procedures (cataract, knee). [1]
Prudent pricing or better-than-expected claims management negotiations. [1]
Claim delays or inadequate provisioning (IBNR) improperly showing profits. [1]
Expense Losses: Lower volumes than expected spreading fixed costs over fewer policies. [1]
Underwriting costs not priced appropriately due to competitive pressure. [1]
High commission payouts or exceptional one-off costs (systems/regulations). [1]
Fraud, leakage, or lack of vendor management. [1]

iv.question: Discuss how the components of the experience analysis will be impacted by: a) Free health check, b) Recapture from reinsurer, c) Group PMI. (4 each)

a) Free Health Check: Morbidity: Detection of hidden issues leads to higher incidence but lower per-claim cost. [1]
Expenses: Costs of check-up increase, but higher sales may lower per-policy fixed costs. [1]
Lapses: Incentive to renew lowers lapse rates; counteracts porting out. [1]
NB: Expected to increase volumes significantly. [1]
b) Recapture: Morbidity: Loss of technical support/data might worsen long-term pricing and experience. [1]
Expenses: Savings on reinsurance premiums, but may need consultants/extra capital. [1]
Lapses: No direct impact as reinsurer isn't customer-facing. [1]
Investment: Retain more profits for risky investment, but higher capital requirements. [1]
c) Group PMI: Morbidity: Experience rated; concentration risk vs market competitiveness. [1]
Expenses: Economies of scale lower per-policy cost; requires new systems. [1]
Lapses: Higher volatility as entire groups enter or exit. [1]
Overall: Lower per-policy profit but higher overall scale. [1]

v.question: Discuss how you would calculate the additional cost of providing this additional benefit under the policy.

Calculation Method: Compare total premium with and without the benefit. [1]
Assumptions: take-up rate, cost of check-up (with medical inflation), revised lapse rates. [2]
Allow for expected new business increase due to the benefit. [1]
Express cost as a % of original premium. [1]

vi.question: Discuss how will the company validate whether the fee charged for recapture is appropriate or not?

Validation Method: Check recapture clause in the treaty for methodology/fee terms. [1]
Calculate Value in Force (VIF) of the reinsured book. [1]
Project reinsurance cash flows using realistic assumptions (morbidity, persistency, tax). [2]
Discount back using Risk Free Rate plus risk/profit margins. [1]

vii.question: Discuss why will the Company choose to offer the policy to large groups only?

Reasons for Large Groups: Economies of scale and lower administration via one master policy. [1]
Avoid anti-selection: group not formed solely for insurance. [1]
Easier to customize benefits and experience rate (less volatility). [2]

i.question: Describe how you would build a medical inflation index.

Index Construction: Choose base year (e.g., historical year with credible data) with index = 100. [1]
Split claim cost into groups; remove outliers/one-offs/epidemics. [1]
Obtain average cost per group; split into hospital, doctor, drugs, equipment, others. [2]
Index = change in weighted component costs between observation periods. [2]
Factors Driving Index: Demand Factors: ageing, awareness, disease pattern shifts (chronic), economic cycles. [3]
Supply Factors: undersupply of doctors, new technologies/procedures, hospital availability. [3]
Actuarial judgement is key to projecting future index values. [2]

ii.question: Evaluate proposal: Product Design, Pricing, Underwriting & Claims, Sales & Marketing, etc.

Product Design: Solves coverage gap from inflation; SI increases irrespective of claims. [1]
Risk of anti-selective lapse if healthy lives face double increase (SI + age). [2]
Over-insurance risk if medical inflation > salary inflation. [1]
NCB can be used as premium reduction to offset SI cost. [1]
Pricing: Project next-year index value and its impact on claim cost vs SI. [2]
Volatility management: use banded approach for SI changes. [1]
Price for NCB impact on renewal and quantify product development expenses. [2]
Reinsurance rates may need resetting; synergy with existing products. [2]
Underwriting & Claims: Sum Under Consideration should include future potential increases. [2]
Increased proportion of medically underwritten business due to higher virtual SI. [2]
No anti-selective behavior as increase is mandatory/indexed. [1]
Sales & Marketing: Best suited for agents/brokers due to complexity. [1]
Remuneration should reflect increased sales time/complexity. [1]
Direct channels for lead gen; target educated profile customers. [1]
Aggressive marketing required to capture early market share. [2]
Administration: Highly complex: needs index capture, SI re-indexing, and new premium engines. [2]

iii.question: Would you recommend the company to launch this product? Provide arguments.

Recommendation Arguments: Recommend if: Market survey shows strong demand for inflation protection. [1]
Arguments: Novelty factor, competitive edge, reset mechanism for future inflation. [2]
Caveats: Anti-selection risk, complex admin costs, potential cannibalization of profitable products. [2]
